data.title: ​Silver Price Retreats Toward $85 After CPI Keeps Rates And Oil In Focus
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) traded firmer on Thursday, March 12, holding near $87 after a bruising midweek reversal, as buyers returned to the market but stopped short of forcing a full breakout. The metal found some support after Wednesday’s slide, yet the broader setup remained tense with oil back near triple digits, the dollar close to its strongest levels of the year, and Treasury yields still elevated.

Silver is trying to rebuild after failing to hold the push toward $89 earlier this week. Thursday’s trade put the market back on firmer footing, but the rebound still looks more like stabilization than a decisive turn, with the recent high area still acting as the first meaningful resistance zone.

Support now comes into view around $85, with a lower floor near $84.50 after that level drew buyers during the latest washout. If silver stays above that band, the market may keep settling into a tighter short-term range. A move back through $88 would improve the near-term picture, though buyers would still need to clear the recent upper boundary to regain stronger control.

The chart remains lively because silver is still trading far above where it started the year. In that kind of environment, failed rallies matter more than usual, since they can quickly pull momentum traders back into profit-taking.

When the bounce reaches the first test

Silver is trying to rebuild after failing to hold the push toward $89 earlier this week. Thursday’s trade put the market back on firmer footing, but the rebound still looks more like stabilization than a decisive turn, with the recent high area still acting as the first meaningful resistance zone.

Support now comes into view around $85, with a lower floor near $84.50 after that level drew buyers during the latest washout. If silver stays above that band, the market may keep settling into a tighter short-term range. A move back through $88 would improve the near-term picture, though buyers would still need to clear the recent upper boundary to regain stronger control. The chart remains lively because silver is still trading far above where it started the year. In that kind of environment, failed rallies matter more than usual, since they can quickly pull momentum traders back into profit-taking.

Macro pressure has not left the room

The inflation picture offered little fresh relief. February consumer prices rose 2.4% from a year earlier, while core inflation held at 2.5%, leaving a reading that was calm enough to avoid a new shock but not soft enough to change the broader rate story. Energy prices kept the market on edge. Oil surged again on Thursday, with Brent (CO1:COM) rising toward $100 and briefly moving higher, as renewed disruption concerns in the Gulf added to fears that energy could keep feeding inflation expectations even after this week’s consumer price report.

At the same time, the dollar stayed close to its 2026 highs and the U.S. 10-year yield remained above 4.20%. That combination does not rule out gains in silver, but it does make follow-through harder unless the market gets either calmer energy pricing or some relief in rates.

What could shape the next leg

If silver keeps holding above $85 and works back into the upper $87-88 area, the market could start looking more balanced after the latest shakeout. That would likely need a steadier oil market or some easing in yields so buyers are not fighting several macro headwinds at once. If crude stays hot and the dollar remains firm, silver may struggle to do much more than bounce inside a volatile range. A move back under $84.50 would put the recent correction back at the center of the chart and raise the risk that the market spends more time cooling before trying higher levels again.

Silver is still trading far above where it began the year, even after this week’s sharp pullback, which is why short-term volatility is likely to remain elevated. The current move matters because it will help determine whether the latest drop was a fast reset inside a larger uptrend or the start of a broader cooling phase.

data.title: Netskope: IPO Lockup Expiration Gift
data.description:
Netskope, Inc. (NTSK) is a leading AI cybersecurity and networking company, yet the stock has slumped after earnings. The company had a hot IPO, but the stock trades at the lowest levels after going public despite the strong demand for their products. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish as the lock-up expiration dip creates a strong buying opportunity.

Lock-Up Expiration

After a company goes public, a stock goes through a volatile period with the initial quarterly results and guidance not always gelling with analyst expectations. In addition, the lock-up expiration after six months tends to lead to a sell-off, with the market pre-empting potential insider sales, with Netscape having ~390 million shares hitting the market on March 13.

Netskope just reported the second quarterly results since going public, and the market potentially didn't fully grasp the sequential expectations. The AI networking company reported the January quarterly results beat analyst estimates by $7 million following the original quarter with an $8 million beat. The stock went public back on September 18, raising $908 million with the offering priced at $19.

The stock surged to open at $23 and hit a high of nearly $28 within days. Ironically, Netskope is now trading below $10 following another good quarter where results beat analyst expectations.

Netskope is at the center of the AI revolution with products managing networking security and analytics covering AI products, especially agentic traffic. The company helps speed up AI traffic while safeguarding a business from cyber threats.

The company reported FQ4 revenue grew by 32% to reach $196 million. Netskope even generated a record net new ARR of $57 million during the January quarter, with total ARR up 31% YoY to reach $811 million.

The only potential issue is the market not understanding guidance. Netskope guided to both FQ1 revenue of $198 million and FY27 revenue of $873 million, both above consensus estimates.

The market has a way to parse data differently once presented in a quarterly report. Netskope has a few potential negatives the market is sweating as follows:

  • FQ1'27 revenue guidance is relatively flat sequentially at $198 million versus $196 million in the prior quarter.
  • FY27 operating margin forecast is highly negative at (10.0%).
  • FY27 revenue guidance at $873 million is a substantial deceleration to 23% growth.
  • Shift to annual billings impacts free cash flows in FY27.
  • The company mentioned macroeconomic risks with FY27 guidance.

The big issue here is that the market really has no grasp on whether the management team provides conservative guidance or not. If the company keeps a tradition of $7 to $8 million quarterly revenue beats, the stock market wouldn't have a big problem with FQ1 revenues of $205+ million.

50% IPO Discount

The stock value has slipped to below $4 billion, with Netskope dipping to nearly $9. The IPO lock-up expiration takes place in the next week, which should free up the stock for less downside pressure, especially considering insiders are unlikely to unload shares at $10 when the IPO took place at $19.

Netskope has net cash of ~$480 million, making the EV closer to $3 billion. The stock only trades at 3x EV/S targets for FY28 with sales forecasts of $1.06 billion, a nearly unheard-of valuation in an AI security and networking company with 20%+ growth.

The stock trades at a big discount to other cybersecurity plays in the networking security market. As mentioned above, Netskope trades at close to 3x EV/S targets, while Zscaler (ZS) trades at 6x forward EV/S targets, and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) is still up at 10x targets, even after the market sell-off in cybersecurity stocks.

The biggest risk is Netskope not hitting financial targets. The company still has large operating losses, and the market will punish the stock with decelerating growth while still unprofitable.

Netskope is cash flow positive, with F26 cash flows from operations at $38 million, but the guidance is to start the year with negative cash flows. The company is forecast for a free cash flow margin of 2% to 4% for FY27, or the equivalent of ~$26 million, more than double the $12 million last year despite the impact from the shift to annual billings.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Netskope sits in an appealing segment with network security becoming a huge focus of AI traffic exploding in an agent world. The market is getting a gift of a hot IPO to trade at a 50% discount while ARR is still growing at a 30%+ rate.

data.title: EWS: Singapore Is The Artificial Intelligence Weathervane We All Need To Monitor
data.description:
The wind always blows, but with artificial intelligence, the current is ever shifting and fracturing. Will an AI bubble pop? Is AI nothing but smoke and mirrors? Will AI usher in a new epoch? Could AI solve the world’s myriad problems? Maybe, possibly, nobody knows. If anyone does know, they’re likely in an inner circle that retail investors aren’t privy to. That said, investors can watch for secondary signals, and I believe Singapore offers one of the best opportunities to do so. Singapore is becoming a leader in AI adoption, and the city-state’s highly competent government is putting its full weight behind AI initiatives.

I’ve covered the iShares MSCI Singapore ETF (EWS) before and rated it a Buy back in July 2025. EWS enjoyed strong gains for a while before cooling off. It was on the rise once again before the war with Iran kicked off. The ETF cooled from about $29.5 to $27 but has started to tick upward again. Amid the intensifying Iran conflict, as a trading hub and city-state, Singapore is in a particularly vulnerable position in many ways. As global tradewinds go, so too will Singapore. I believe the price decline right now is temporary and largely due to the war with Iran. However, I also suspect that conditions will get worse before they get better, but at the same time, I don’t expect the war to continue for an overly prolonged period of time.

Anyway, I rate Singapore a Buy and believe that AI will deliver substantial productivity gains, which will bolster Singaporean companies. I also believe that if AI does supercharge the economy and productivity in Singapore, other countries will be able to find similar success, fueling increasing adoption, which in turn will propel the AI industry as a whole. If Singapore struggles to substantially boost productivity, it could suggest that AI is smoke and mirrors, or at the very least, not ready to change the world. Singapore’s small size, high adoption rate, and effective governance make it a perfect test bed.

Before jumping into AI, however, I’d like to quickly cover some important historical details.

Singapore Ranks Among The Best-Managed Countries The World Over

Singapore has the unique distinction of being one of the few countries ever to be forcefully pushed out of its motherland. The colonial history is rather complex, but to simplify things, Singapore was used by the British as a trading and transport hub and was administered separately from the nearby Malay Peninsula. The British colonized Singapore in 1819 and took a generally light-handed approach with the native bumiputera (sons of the soil) across much of what is now Malaysia. In Singapore, rather than relying heavily on locals, the British encouraged Chinese traders to move to the island. (The British would make deeper inroads into peninsular Malaysia primarily in the latter half of the 19th century.)

Anyway, Singapore became a Chinese-dominated city, but it was attached to a Bumiputera (primarily Malay) motherland. As the British wound down their colonial empire after World War II, Singapore and the rest of Malaysia formed a federation. However, ethnic tensions between Chinese and Malays, and especially between the Malay-dominated federal government and the Chinese-dominated Singaporean government, led to Singapore being pushed out.

This is important because as soon as Singapore was pushed out, it found itself in an extremely difficult position. It had no motherland, its market was tiny, and its natural resources were scarce. This sparked an existential drive that would see Singapore abandon traditional development models, such as import substitution. In many ways, Singapore laid down the framework for globalization that would later reshape the world. The government invested heavily in key areas, like manufacturing, real estate development, and air travel, with an emphasis on exports, but was also careful to ensure a competitive market economy, thus helping prevent wasteful government projects.

Singapore still views itself as vulnerable, and the government still plays a very active role in society while emphasizing efficiency and working to avoid bloat. The government emphasizes meritocracy and offers salaries competitive with the private sector as well. In the 2010 American diplomat cables, you can find plenty of praise for the Singaporean government and its management of society and the economy. I believe this is important, because if I had to bet on a government making a successful society-wide push to encourage AI adoption, Singapore would be at the top of my list.

Singapore Blazing The Artificial Intelligence Trail

Singapore was one of the trailblazers in regard to modern development and globalization. Now, it’s aiming to pave a path with Artificial Intelligence. The city-state constantly needs to maintain a competitive edge and is surrounded by neighbors who can offer cheaper labor and more natural resources while generally being well-managed to some extent or another. The country’s population is aging and shrinking as well. These challenges encourage the Singaporean government to remain nimble and forward-looking.

The Singaporean government is working to create at least 100,000 AI bilingual workers over the next few years, meaning people with both AI skills and skills in other fields. So, you have AI bilingual accountants and legal professionals. While teachers in many countries are worried about students using AI to cheat, Singapore is setting up programs to teach kids and college students how to effectively use AI. The government is also providing free subscriptions for citizens taking select courses to test out AI.

As for businesses, the Enterprise Innovation Scheme will provide up to 400% tax deductions for AI-related expenses for businesses with a cap of up to 50,000 SGD. A forthcoming “Champions of AI Initiative” will see the government working closely alongside Singaporean companies to foster AI-related leadership skills and upskill workers. When I see governments launch these sorts of programs, I temper my expectations because they frequently fall short, but the Singaporean government has a proven track record, and if anyone can pull it off, it’s probably them.

As a result of various efforts, Singapore has, by some measures, become the world’s leader in terms of AI adoption. Cybernews ranks Singapore number 1 globally and notes that adoption has grown from 24% of citizens using AI in 2023 to 52% by 2024 and 66% by 2025. By adoption, I mean getting employees and citizens to use AI, getting management buy-in, and producing concrete results, rather than conducting research in bleeding-edge models like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO).

Singaporean businesses are reporting a 16% return on investment (ROI) with AI projects and are aiming to hit 29% within the next few years. 38% are expecting to increase AI investments, and 67% are satisfied with the outcomes of their AI projects so far. AI adoption is still rather nascent, and AI technology itself is probably rudimentary compared to what we’ll have access to in 10 years. That early returns are so promising, however, bodes well not just for Singapore but for the AI industry as well. I believe Singaporean companies will deliver on long-term productivity gains, which in turn makes EWS an especially attractive investment.

EWS: Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

The first thing that jumps out to me with EWS is the low portfolio P/E ratio, which, according to Morningstar, weighs in at ~15.3. This P/S ratio is also quite low at ~2.3. These valuation metrics are often quite high in the United States, leading some to speculate if the stock market is getting a bit bubbly. The holdings breakdown, as shown below, suggests that this ETF is rather highly concentrated in financials. I’m a bit torn on this. I generally like to see more diversity. However, with the AI thesis, if Singapore can secure productivity gains, it should stimulate general business activity, which in turn should bolster the financial sector.

The adjusted expense ratio is quite low at .5%. I’ve reviewed a fair number of overseas ETFs, and this is one of the lower adjusted expense ratios I’ve seen. Ratios of roughly .6% or higher are pretty common. The dividend yield, as shown below, is quite attractive, topping 4%, with the 3-year CAGR quite high at 32.77%. If the economy hits a rough patch, I wouldn’t be surprised if dividends shrank rather quickly as companies circle the wagons and try to preserve cash.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant tools have been cooling on EWS. For many months, the ETF was in the green with a Buy recommendation (shown below). Recently, it slipped into Hold territory and then into Sell territory. Yet the individual grades aren’t bad but rather mediocre. Overall, I disagree with the quant tools grades, especially in light of the AI thesis. It can be hard to capture forward-looking potential with quant tools, and in a sense, I think that’s what’s happening here.

Why I Could Be Wrong

For one, Artificial Intelligence could run into some sort of ceiling. While progress has been rapid so far, we can’t really be sure of the upside. As for EWS being a Buy, Singapore is in a rather vulnerable position, and the war with Iran, as well as global trade tensions, could have an outsized impact on the city-state. As for general views of artificial intelligence, it’s possible that Singapore will turn out to be an exception rather than a rule regarding AI adoption. Perhaps Singapore will succeed while others will fail.

Key Takeaway: Keep An Eye On Singapore And A Buy On EWS

EWS offers a good mix of fundamentals and diversification from American equities. In a sense, Singapore as a whole can be viewed as an AI play, given how quickly the country is adapting and leveraging AI. Even if you’re not interested in investing in Singapore, I still recommend keeping an eye on how AI adoption goes, as it could portend the future for many other countries.

data.title: The Recent Pullback In Hingham Institution for Savings Seems Justified
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to my past analysis of Hingham Institution for Savings (HIFS). When I first wrote about the company in November of last year, I rated its stock as a Hold based on its elevated valuation and risk profile. Since that article was published, HIFS has risen about 7.5% while the overall market has only achieved a gain of about 2.5%. Hingham had been able to post even greater gains through early February of this year, but the stock has tumbled almost 17% since reaching a 52-week high about five weeks ago.

Much of the decline in the stock can be explained by macro factors in the market. Nonetheless, I do think that the recent pullback in HIFS is justified based on the most recent quarterly results posted by the company. Hingham’s low net interest margin and below-average operational metrics simply make it a less attractive investment option compared to other small regional banks that I have covered on this site in the last six months. I am maintaining my Hold rating for HIFS.

Company Profile

Hingham Institution for Savings is one of the oldest banks in the US, with a history that dates back almost 200 years. The bank has a rather unique geographic footprint with six branches in Massachusetts, one in San Francisco, and one in the nation’s capital of Washington, DC. Hingham has several unique services for its customers that include no fees for active accounts and unlimited deposit insurance thanks to the Massachusetts Depositors Insurance Fund.

The company has a current market cap of $610.5 million. According to the 2025 10-K filed by HIFS on March 4, Hingham’s total assets were valued at $4.54 billion at the end of last year, a modest increase of 1.9% from the end of 2024. Included in those assets were gross loans outstanding worth $3.93 billion. A full snapshot of the bank’s balance sheet data from the company’s 2025 Annual Report is shown below.

As I mentioned in my first analysis for HIFS, I have never seen another bank so committed to real estate, with 84.1% of its loan mix tied to commercial real estate and an additional 11.1% connected to home mortgages. An additional 4.0% is committed to construction loans. Non-real estate consumer and commercial loans make up less than 1% of the bank’s portfolio.

Total deposits for the bank totaled $2.59 billion at the end of Q4. The company relies heavily on wholesale funding to grow its loans. Hingham reported $1.46 in advances from the Federal Home Loan Bank at the end of last year. The average bank of a similar size to HIFS has a ratio of net loans to total deposits of 84.46%, according to the FDIC. For Hingham, that ratio is 153.0%.

The company pays a regular quarterly dividend of $0.63, an amount that has not changed since the last quarter of 2022. However, Hingham also has a history of supplementing its regular payouts with special dividends, an event that took place in January of this year when the company paid its stockholders an additional $0.70. This was the first special distribution for the company since the final quarter of 2022. When the company did not make an additional payout in 2023, it marked the first time that Hingham had not done so since 2010. Without the inclusion of the special dividend, HIFS has a forward yield of only 0.90%.

Analysis of Hingham Institution for Savings

When looking at the bottom line for HIFS in FY 2025, the company enjoyed a banner year. Basic earnings per share of $25.01 were almost double the $12.95 earned by the company in FY 2024. However, not all of that growth was operational. For example, the company reported a gain on the sale of equity securities in Q3 2025 that added $4.03 to the bottom line for HIFS in that quarter.

The company’s ability to profit from the sale of securities is not a one-time occurrence. Hingham reported a net gain on equity securities of $20.4 million in FY 2024 and followed that performance with $28.8 million in gains in FY 2025. Considering the total net income for the bank before taxes was $72.8 million, these security sales have been a meaningful contributor to Hingham's bottom line.

These sales are also clouding the true operational performance of the bank itself. Hingham reported a return on assets of 1.84% for Q4 2025 and 1.22% for the full year. The quarterly number is well above the national average for banks of its size. However, when security sales are removed from the equation, ROA drops to 0.89% for Q4 and 0.72% for FY 2025. The national average for this metric in Q4 2025 was 1.31%, showing that the actual banking operations for HIFS are not producing the same returns as typical peer banks. A similar impact on return on equity can be seen in the chart below. As a point of reference, the national average for ROE in Q4 was 11.88%.

The outstanding efficiency ratio posted by the bank in Q4 was not impacted by the securities sale. The Q4 ratio of 35.06% is well below the national mean and an improvement of over 17% from the same quarter in 2024. This is an impressive achievement by the company. However, the strong performance in holding down expenses was offset by a net interest margin that is over 50% below the national average for a bank of Hingham’s asset size.

The net interest margin of 1.89% that was reported at the end of the year was being impacted by both low loan yields and high costs for its deposits. HIFS has a loan portfolio that was yielding just 4.82% at the end of last year. The typical bank of Hingham’s size had a loan yield of over 6.5%. The bank reported a cost of interest-bearing deposits of 3.15% at the end of 2025, 46 basis points higher than the national average.

When all the pieces are added together, the banking operations for Hingham Institution for Savings are not as strong as they appear from just a quick glance at the recent profit numbers. The PE ratio for the bank of 11.3 is slightly higher than I prefer in a small-cap bank. With a tangible book value of $219.82, the stock is trading at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.24, which is not exactly in bargain territory for a bank with the substandard performance metrics mentioned earlier.

Additional Risks to Consider

When compared to the end of 2024, the asset quality for Hingham Institution for Savings has taken a noticeable downward fall. The ratio of non-performing loans to total loans stood at 0.80% at the end of 2025, a deterioration of 76 basis points over the last 12 months. Non-performing assets reached 0.69%, 66 basis points worse than at the end of FY 2024. Much of this increase is tied to one $30.6 million CRE loan that was placed on non-accrual status last year.

The bank’s allowance for credit losses has only increased by four basis points since the end of 2024, giving reason to believe that the bank may have to account for additional loan issues in the future should other loans on its books deteriorate. However, the bank has a tradition of strong lending practices, and it should be noted that HIFS has been able to avoid any charge-offs in FY 2025 or FY 2024.

The company also had to respond to a report from a short seller last month that stated the bank is “over-leveraged” and “under-reserved.” This report identified over $125 million in loans in the Washington, DC, area that may not be properly assessed as distressed. The bank has disputed these claims and affirmed the accuracy of its financial reports.

Even without the concerns that were mentioned in this report, there is no denying that Hingham has an almost 100% exposure to real estate in its loan portfolio. This makes the bank especially sensitive to issues that could make the repayment of those loans, both commercial and consumer, more challenging in the future. These issues could include rising unemployment, increasing consumer debt, a slowdown in the growth of the US economy, and stubborn inflation that unexpectedly moves higher later this year.

The bank’s quirky service area of Massachusetts, DC, and San Francisco also makes it susceptible to issues that could impact those markets specifically. According to the company’s 2025 10-K, 63.5% of the loans at the bank are secured by properties in Massachusetts. An additional 33.1% are linked to properties in Washington, DC. Recent estimates show that Massachusetts is trailing the national average for GDP growth, a trend that has been in place for the last couple of years. The causes for this include higher taxes, a rising cost of living, and resident migration to other states. Washington, DC, has also faced economic headwinds over the last year related to government cutbacks.

There is still a great deal of skepticism for the company as a whole. When I wrote my first analysis for HIFS, the stock had a short interest of 11.68%. On March 12, that number had risen to 15.71%. Whether it be the bank's low core metrics, asset quality issues, or lack of growth opportunities in its service areas, there are large bets in the market that this stock is likely to underperform in the near future.

The Bottom Line

Hingham Institution for Savings has shown some modest improvement in its overall picture since my last analysis was published. However, the valuation of its stock seems to imply a higher level of banking performance than what I have found. That fact, added in with the loan risks, is enough for me to avoid an upgrade at this time. Even with the special dividend included, its stock’s yield of 1.16% is not enough for me to wait around for these issues to improve. The short interest also raises a red flag that there may be more troubles lying ahead for the bank. I am maintaining my Hold rating on HIFS with better options available in the small-cap regional bank space.

data.title: Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Large Cap Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The underperformance of the Quality factor, which the Fund tilts toward, was a headwind to relative performance this quarter. The best performing sectors for the Russell 1000 index were Health Care and Communications Services, while Real Estate and Utilities were the weakest sectors.

During the quarter, sector allocation and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance. An underweight to both Information Technology and Real Estate aided performance, while an underweight Health Care and overweight Financials detracted from performance.

Turning to stock selection, the best performing stocks based on relative performance vs. the benchmark during the quarter included Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (Communications Services sector), FedEx Corp. (FDX) (Industrials sector), and Chubb Ltd. (CB) (Financials sector).

Alphabet was a top performer this quarter following strong core business execution and accelerating adoption of its AI offerings. Growth was broad-based across search, YouTube, and cloud, with AI enhancing search utility and monetization while cloud margins reached new highs. Management continues to balance disciplined cost controls with increased AI investment, reinforcing confidence in long-term returns. We remain attracted to its massive ecosystem scale, sound capital allocation, and clean balance sheet.

FedEx was a top performer after reporting better results led by improved revenue quality, disciplined pricing, and continued cost savings. Despite the freight recession, domestic ground volume has been fairly resilient recently, and margins expanded in the core Express business due to the structural cost reductions. We remain attracted to the strong cash generation, balanced capital allocation, and a solid balance sheet.

Chubb was a strong performer in the fourth quarter, reaching an all-time high, following exceptional earnings that featured record core operating income, industry-leading combined ratios, and robust investment income growth. The company’s diversified portfolio, disciplined underwriting, and aggressive capital returns reinforced confidence in its ability to generate attractive returns, even as the Property and Casualty industry transitions to a more competitive and volatile environment.

The more challenged positions based on relative performance during the quarter included Fiserv Inc. (FISV) (Financials sector), NewMarket Corp. (NEU) (Materials sector), and BlackRock Inc. (BLK) (Financial Services sector).

Fiserv weakness came after ongoing concerns about a slowdown in its small business point-of-sale platform on top of weak third quarter results. The company took a steep guidance cut and announced management and Board changes. The large stock reaction reflects a loss of credibility, increased doubts around the long-term growth outlook, and the realization that a turnaround will require meaningful investment over the medium-term. Given the extent of the reset and diminished confidence in the thesis, we sold our position.

NewMarket was a weaker performer as a softer global environment pressured Petroleum Additives volumes and margins and the Specialty Materials business had lumpy demand, which weighed on results. Lower oil prices reduced operating leverage and drove an unusual restructuring charge as industry capacity increased.

Despite near-term pressure, we continue to view NewMarket as a strong business with disciplined capital allocation, balance sheet flexibility, and strong cash flow generation. Attracted to its best-in- class operations, conservative underwriting, and shareholder- friendly capital allocation philosophy.

BlackRock was a bottom performer during the quarter as investor sentiment was pressured by concerns around private market liquidity, return durability, and higher expenses tied to recent acquisitions. Despite this, fundamentals remain solid, with record AUM, healthy flows, and organic base fee growth well above long-term targets, driven by active ETFs, digital assets, and alternatives. We continue to view BlackRock as a long-term share gainer, and we appreciate the strong balance sheet and steady capital return.

In late December, we trimmed the large position in Alphabet Inc. based on strength and to comply with our stated 10% position limits. We used the funds to increase our position in Republic Services Inc. (RSG) (Industrials sector).

We added to Republic Services as it is an attractive opportunity to increase our exposure in a defensive and resilient business model. The waste management industry has lagged the broader market this year due to cyclical volume weakness in construction, demolition, and the industrial sector. Despite these short term headwinds, Republic Services continues to exhibit operational strength through pricing power and cost controls, which have led to stronger margins. We remain attracted to the high-quality, essential business with strong and stable cash flow generation.

Recently, insiders have bought shares in the open market, which we view as an additional data point to the attractive valuation.

In October, we sold the remaining Fiserv position after it hit our soft stop loss. The doubts over the new long-term algorithm and the durability of its core business are now in question. Prior management’s reliance on low-quality, short-term growth led us to lose confidence in the business. While there is still value in the core business, it is now a turnaround story that will require heavy investment to reset the strategy and restore genuine earnings growth.

In October, we used the excess cash to fund two purchases of existing holdings, Chubb Ltd. and Norfolk Southern Corp. (NSC) (Industrials sector).

We added to our Chubb position after the most recent quarterly report. Results continue to be better than expected and management increased its return on equity target. Loss trends in the industry have stabilized and premiums written should accelerate. Management also accelerated the buyback program and signaled that the stock is trading under its intrinsic value. We remain confident in the attractive nature Chubb business.

We added to our Norfolk Southern position after the shares pulled back despite the pending Union Pacific deal. It is currently trading at a discount to the value of the Union Pacific deal and the rail industry is out of favor. While most recent earnings were softer than expected Norfolk Southern has shown signs of improving its execution and volumes should return incrementally. Lastly, the chairman stepped up and purchased shares in the open market, which we view as a sign of conviction in the business.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of large-cap companies which may be unable to respond quickly to new competitive challenges. data.title: Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Utilizes a bottom-up security selection process that screens potential investments against a proprietary quantitative model for return on capital, earnings to value ratio, free cash flow and return on equity
  • Looks at a company’s corporate governance structure and management incentives to try to ascertain whether or not management’s interests are aligned with shareholder interests
  • Seeks to identify the sources of a company’s competitive advantage as well as what levers management has at its disposal to increase shareholder value
  • Seeks to purchase generally profitable, financially stable large-cap companies that consistently generate high returns on unleveraged operating capital, are run by shareholder-oriented management, and are trading at a discount to their private market value

Market Recap

U.S. equities finished higher for a third consecutive quarter, led by a shift towards a more accommodative monetary policy, resilient consumer spending, and better-than-expected earnings results. Clarity in trade policy also helped ease uncertainties. The 10-year Treasury yield increased to 4.2% amid sticky inflation and delayed expectations of further U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cuts.

Economic data during the quarter were mixed but generally better than expected, reflecting an economy that remained resilient. Real GDP accelerated to a 4.3% annualized pace in the third quarter, providing a strong starting point for fourth quarter, supported by solid consumer spending and business investment. However, estimates are showing a moderation in growth expectations going forward, alongside modest labor market softening and sticky inflation. Affordability pressures and labor market concerns weighed on consumer sentiment. Despite these headwinds and a government shutdown, earnings results remained supportive, reinforcing a constructive fundamental backdrop even as economic momentum began to cool.

Near-term data continues to be delayed by the government shutdown, but the most recent data showed signs of progress being made on the inflation side. Inflation surprised to the downside in November, with core CPI cooling more than expected. The most recent Core PCE, the Fed’s preferred gauge (which excludes food and energy), has not been updated since September. All that said, inflation remains above the Fed’s target.

The labor market data showed unemployment increased and has continued to soften. Labor demand has fallen more than supply, which has been a main driver for the unemployment rate ticking higher, not necessarily due to layoffs. On a positive note, productivity also increased significantly. Unemployment rates were 4.6%. The weakening unemployment numbers continue to be a bigger risk to the overall economy.

Regarding monetary policy, the Fed maintained its restrictive monetary policy but lowered the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to 3.5-3.75% in December due to the rising downside risk of higher unemployment. The three rate cuts this year were all framed as a risk management move to bring policy closer to neutral. The Fed is trying to execute a careful balancing act with the decision to cut rates despite still elevated and potentially persistent inflation. Fed projections now show only one 2026 cut, which may be close to the neutral rate. Additional easing is possible depending on economic data throughout the year.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Large Cap Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The underperformance of the Quality factor, which the Fund tilts toward, was a headwind to relative performance this quarter. The best performing sectors for the Russell 1000 index were Health Care and Communications Services, while Real Estate and Utilities were the weakest sectors.

During the quarter, sector allocation and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance. An underweight to both Information Technology and Real Estate aided performance, while an underweight Health Care and overweight Financials detracted from performance.

Turning to stock selection, the best performing stocks based on relative performance vs. the benchmark during the quarter included Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (Communications Services sector), FedEx Corp. (FDX) (Industrials sector), and Chubb Ltd. (CB) (Financials sector).

Alphabet was a top performer this quarter following strong core business execution and accelerating adoption of its AI offerings. Growth was broad-based across search, YouTube, and cloud, with AI enhancing search utility and monetization while cloud margins reached new highs. Management continues to balance disciplined cost controls with increased AI investment, reinforcing confidence in long-term returns. We remain attracted to its massive ecosystem scale, sound capital allocation, and clean balance sheet.

FedEx was a top performer after reporting better results led by improved revenue quality, disciplined pricing, and continued cost savings. Despite the freight recession, domestic ground volume has been fairly resilient recently, and margins expanded in the core Express business due to the structural cost reductions. We remain attracted to the strong cash generation, balanced capital allocation, and a solid balance sheet.

Chubb was a strong performer in the fourth quarter, reaching an all-time high, following exceptional earnings that featured record core operating income, industry-leading combined ratios, and robust investment income growth. The company’s diversified portfolio, disciplined underwriting, and aggressive capital returns reinforced confidence in its ability to generate attractive returns, even as the Property and Casualty industry transitions to a more competitive and volatile environment.

The more challenged positions based on relative performance during the quarter included Fiserv Inc. (FISV) (Financials sector), NewMarket Corp. (NEU) (Materials sector), and BlackRock Inc. (BLK) (Financial Services sector).

Fiserv weakness came after ongoing concerns about a slowdown in its small business point-of-sale platform on top of weak third quarter results. The company took a steep guidance cut and announced management and Board changes. The large stock reaction reflects a loss of credibility, increased doubts around the long-term growth outlook, and the realization that a turnaround will require meaningful investment over the medium-term. Given the extent of the reset and diminished confidence in the thesis, we sold our position.

NewMarket was a weaker performer as a softer global environment pressured Petroleum Additives volumes and margins and the Specialty Materials business had lumpy demand, which weighed on results. Lower oil prices reduced operating leverage and drove an unusual restructuring charge as industry capacity increased.

Despite near-term pressure, we continue to view NewMarket as a strong business with disciplined capital allocation, balance sheet flexibility, and strong cash flow generation. Attracted to its best-in- class operations, conservative underwriting, and shareholder- friendly capital allocation philosophy.

BlackRock was a bottom performer during the quarter as investor sentiment was pressured by concerns around private market liquidity, return durability, and higher expenses tied to recent acquisitions. Despite this, fundamentals remain solid, with record AUM, healthy flows, and organic base fee growth well above long-term targets, driven by active ETFs, digital assets, and alternatives. We continue to view BlackRock as a long-term share gainer, and we appreciate the strong balance sheet and steady capital return.

In late December, we trimmed the large position in Alphabet Inc. based on strength and to comply with our stated 10% position limits. We used the funds to increase our position in Republic Services Inc. (RSG) (Industrials sector).

We added to Republic Services as it is an attractive opportunity to increase our exposure in a defensive and resilient business model. The waste management industry has lagged the broader market this year due to cyclical volume weakness in construction, demolition, and the industrial sector. Despite these short term headwinds, Republic Services continues to exhibit operational strength through pricing power and cost controls, which have led to stronger margins. We remain attracted to the high-quality, essential business with strong and stable cash flow generation.

Recently, insiders have bought shares in the open market, which we view as an additional data point to the attractive valuation.

In October, we sold the remaining Fiserv position after it hit our soft stop loss. The doubts over the new long-term algorithm and the durability of its core business are now in question. Prior management’s reliance on low-quality, short-term growth led us to lose confidence in the business. While there is still value in the core business, it is now a turnaround story that will require heavy investment to reset the strategy and restore genuine earnings growth.

In October, we used the excess cash to fund two purchases of existing holdings, Chubb Ltd. and Norfolk Southern Corp. (NSC) (Industrials sector).

We added to our Chubb position after the most recent quarterly report. Results continue to be better than expected and management increased its return on equity target. Loss trends in the industry have stabilized and premiums written should accelerate. Management also accelerated the buyback program and signaled that the stock is trading under its intrinsic value. We remain confident in the attractive nature Chubb business.

We added to our Norfolk Southern position after the shares pulled back despite the pending Union Pacific deal. It is currently trading at a discount to the value of the Union Pacific deal and the rail industry is out of favor. While most recent earnings were softer than expected Norfolk Southern has shown signs of improving its execution and volumes should return incrementally. Lastly, the chairman stepped up and purchased shares in the open market, which we view as a sign of conviction in the business.

Outlook and Conclusion

Despite tariff pressures and a softening labor market, economic growth remained resilient through much of the year, supported by solid consumer spending, productivity, and continued AI-driven investment. Rising productivity increased earnings and fueled broad-based volatility and small-cap equity gains. Elevated valuations now reflect optimistic assumptions around sustained earnings growth and productivity gains. Macro risks have grown more pronounced, including a softening labor market, mounting affordability pressures on consumers, and global policy uncertainty, suggesting markets may be more vulnerable to disappointment as growth slows and policy support becomes less certain.

We expect more modest returns amid elevated valuations, concentrated leadership, and rising macro uncertainty, which increases the risk of volatility and drawdowns. Although we are not forecasting a recession, late-cycle dynamics are becoming more apparent with the uneven economic data. Defensive portfolios like ours remain prudent protection against risks that could still materialize.

In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears likely to hold interest rates steady at its next meeting, with the markets pricing in one additional rate cut later in 2026. Softer job market data has driven this shift, as policymakers aim to prevent further labor market deterioration while inflation remains above target. As policy moves toward neutral, it is important to note that monetary changes take time to impact the economy.

In terms of the equity market, valuations based on near-term earnings and expectations are elevated. This has reduced the margin for error, as seen with recent earnings, generating limited upside for beats and outsized downside for misses. We believe that modest returns with unpredictable downdrafts appear likely for U.S. equities over the next decade. We see the most compelling opportunities in high-quality companies with durable fundamentals, particularly those that generate strong free cash flow, maintain conservative balance sheets, and return capital to shareholders.

Given we appear to be late in the economic cycle, with growth strong but showing signs of moderation, this environment should favor Quality factors and our portfolios over the next few years. Importantly, when Quality outperforms, it tends to be significant, while underperformance during lagging periods is typically more modest. Longer term, we believe that quality attributes and solid company fundamentals will lead to strong risk adjusted returns.

data.title: 12-Week War To Shave 3% Off Global GDP In A Demand Destruction Event
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As we near two weeks of war, there are some definite trends taking shape that are set to impact the global oil market (CL1:COM) in the next few months, and perhaps beyond. First off, despite reassuring rhetoric, it increasingly looks like both sides are digging in for a prolonged conflict, while at the same time, there are fewer and fewer off-ramps for either side, and neither side looks like it is facing imminent and total defeat. The resulting negative impact on the global oil & gas supply/demand balance is starting to take shape as well. It is, in my view, important to quantify it in terms of the magnitude of shortfall in global supplies that we are facing, which is amplified in time, and the demand destruction event that it is likely to trigger. I intend to incrementally reshape my portfolio in a way that reflects the correlation between the length of the war and the impact it has on the market.

The EIA's Baffling Inventory Build Forecast for This Year

As I have been highlighting since 2024, institutional guidance for the global oil markets coming from the likes of the EIA, IEA & OPEC seems to be increasingly lacking in objectivity, with more and more potential bias built in. My last article on the subject highlighted the discrepancy between the EIA's claim that we had a roughly 4 mb/d global supply surplus in Q4, 2025, versus its own data on global monthly oil production. I concluded that a 2 mb/d surplus was a more likely oil market balance situation. Given reports of massive volumes of output being shut in as the Strait of Hormuz remains blocked, that difference of about 2 mb/d between the EIA's guidance and the more likely reality becomes increasingly important.

While past estimates & forecasts were puzzling, the latest report released on March 10 left me with no doubt that providing as accurate guidance as possible to the global oil & energy markets is no longer the driving force behind the work of such institutions.

As we can see, the EIA still expects a slight 1 mb/d inventory build for the current quarter. Given what was already known about the volumes of oil being shut in by OPEC members in response to not being able to have it transported through the Strait, this forecast is impossible to justify, even if we were to assume that the war will end by the middle of March.

It is thought that by March 9, oil output had been slashed by 2.9 mb/d in Iraq, 2 to 2.5 mb/d in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait slashed production by about 500k b/d, and the UAE saw a decline of 500-800k b/d. We can also assume that Iran's output has declined by more or less the equivalent of its export volumes of 1.5 mb/d. The maximum known cuts in output known to the EIA before the report was completed on the 9th of March were about 7-8 mb/d. Even after the war ends and the Strait of Hormuz is fully open, it will take a few months to fully restore all that lost output. It is therefore mathematically impossible for the world to see any inventory build in the current quarter.

A Billion Barrels of Oil Supply Will Be Lost by Summer

In the first week of conflict, probably about 20-30 million barrels were shut in, mostly in Iran & Iraq. This week, it looks like about 50 million barrels, as storage facilities in the Gulf are filling up fast. If the war does not end, we might be on a path toward a daily supply shortfall of as much as 10-12 mb/d, or about 70-80 million barrels/week, as more oil is shut in.

Some oil seems to be getting through, with Saudi Arabia & UAE diverting it to pipelines that avoid the Strait. Iran also has a pipeline that bypasses the Strait, but it is unclear whether it is making use of it. It is also reported that Iran is still shipping some oil to China via the Strait.

Taking all factors into consideration, we can assume that starting next week, we will lose 75 million barrels/week. Therefore, hypothetically, if this war and blockade of the Strait lasts another 10 weeks, we will lose about 750 million barrels, in addition to the roughly 80 million barrels that we lost in the first 2 weeks. Even once the war ends and the Strait is opened, it will probably take about two months for production to be fully restored. During that time, we can assume we will lose another 200 million barrels or so, bringing the total to roughly 1 billion barrels of oil.

I should note that my estimate of probable volumes of output set to be lost in the event that the war lasts a total of 12 weeks is most likely on the conservative side.

In a worst-case scenario, we could potentially start losing close to 20 mb/d in output, which is nearly double my estimate.

Why We Should Expect a Demand Destruction Event Over the 2026-2027 Period

Based on the EIA's quarterly supply/demand forecasts, we can expect to return to an average supply surplus situation of about 3 mb/d, starting in the second half of this year. What this means is that by the end of 2027, the net supply shortfall of about 1 billion barrels would be largely restored in global inventory levels, without a demand destruction event being necessary to make it happen.

In reality, we are likely to see an average decline of about 1 mb/d in global oil consumption this year and next, and that is how we will partially restore global inventories to pre-war levels by 2027. In practice, this means zero growth in global oil consumption this year and next. The mechanism that will make the market rebalancing happen is most likely to be higher oil prices.

The impact on the global economy will be felt in the form of much lower economic growth. I have been using a formula for over a decade that I have not cited for some time now, which illustrates the oil supply to global economic growth relationship. The formula is based on observing long-term correlations, and it goes as follows.

Global GDP growth = 1.5% (efficiency gains) + (oil output or consumption growth x 2)

Entering zero growth in global oil consumption for this year and next into the equation, we are left with 1.5% global GDP growth on average for this year and next.

One could calculate the effect on the next 4 years instead of 2 by spreading out the supply deficit over a longer period, which would allow us to enter .5% oil consumption growth/year. In that case, we would have 2.5% average economic growth for the next four years.

The IMF had global economic growth averaging about 3.3% for these two years; therefore, we can assume that a 12-week war will shave off about 1.8% from global GDP this year & next.

The impact may not be uniform across all the regions represented in the IMF chart. The euro area, for instance, may be particularly hard-hit since it imports most of its oil, and it is also facing a shortfall in natural gas imports because Qatar's LNG is not flowing, and it has shut in production. The oil price differential between America's WTI and Europe's Brent may also widen due to the continent's overexposure to imports. Japan & S. Korea are faced with similar considerations.

The IEA Decision to Release 400 Million Barrels of Oil

As I started writing this article, the IEA just had its members agree to release 400 million barrels from their strategic reserves. I want to point out that this release does not resolve the problem or have much of an impact on the overall demand destruction event we are likely to experience. The total inventory decline of roughly 1 billion barrels is still set to happen; it is just that it will temporarily alleviate the effects on commercial inventories.

The net effect is that it shifts the timeline. In other words, the effect on oil prices will be less severe in the next few months, while the higher prices might stick around for much longer. This is the preferred policy strategy that I alluded to earlier, where total demand destruction will still amount to about 1 billion barrels, but perhaps it will last for the rest of the decade, rather than happen more immediately.

Expected Effects on Oil Prices

My first instinct in trying to assess the probable path of oil prices was to look back at the 2007-2008 oil price spike as a model. After further reflections and keeping in mind policy preference factors, I believe it will be somewhat different from that event.

In the run-up to the 2008 oil price spike, which established a record high for the WTI benchmark, there were two defining factors that created that massive spike in oil prices, followed by an even deeper decline. The oil price spike ran up against central banks that focused on taming inflation resulting from those higher oil prices.

I do not believe that we will see interest rates pushed higher in an effort to fight off the inflationary pressures resulting from higher oil prices this time around. Neither is the economy afflicted with the systemic risks we had back then in the form of the subprime mortgage crisis. The consumer and business are, therefore, arguably in a more resilient position

The other interesting differentiating factor is that even though oil prices were steadily rising, culminating in that massive spike, SPR reserves were never released.

As we can see, SPR inventory levels were more or less steady, even as oil prices were spiking. With the IEA decision, we can expect perhaps half of the 400 million barrels to be released to come from the US. The SPR is set to be significantly depleted within the next few months. It would not surprise me if further drawdown decisions are made within two months or so if the war continues until then.

What we should expect from oil prices for the rest of the duration of this war is a steady upward march. We may or may not see the 2008 record high price of almost $150/barrel, but whatever price level we will ultimately reach, we are likely to see that price level persist for the duration of the war.

After the war ends, we are likely to see a few months of volatility, with prices mostly headed down. Once global oil output is fully restored, we will most likely see bidding to fill inventories from both commercial entities and governments, which will lead to a higher price floor relative to the pre-war period.

Investment Implications

My initial reaction to the conflict was to prepare for a sharp oil price spike, which is a great opportunity to take profits on my ample oil industry stock positions. At the same time, I was expecting an equally sharp broader market selloff to provide ample opportunities to pick up certain stocks at a bargain. I was expecting a sharp selloff in oil once the war ends and a sharp rebound in stock markets.

I no longer see this playing out as predictably as I initially thought. I still intend to wait until week three of this war before trading, just as I announced in an article I wrote over a week ago. My approach will be even more measured and cautious, making only small changes to my portfolio each week going forward.

My core oil & gas positions consist of Suncor (SU), CNQ (CNQ), and Equinor (EQNR). I have smaller positions in Occidental (OXY), Cheniere (LNG), Petroleo Brasiliero (PBR), and EOG (EOG). My non-core positions will be the first to be reduced or completely eliminated, after which I intend to gradually start reducing my core positions. The latter I intend to keep as a sizable portion of my overall portfolio throughout, for the possibility that this war will not end this year as expected. Once oil prices fall significantly in the post-war period, I intend to start buying again.

As far as bargain hunting, I recently announced my intention to look for a good entry position for SQM (SQM) as well as Mosaic (MOS). However, I mostly intend to look for opportunities throughout the year, possibly targeting dozens of different stocks, which is a big difference from my initial assumption that there will be a narrow window of opportunity to pick up bargains.

A word on gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver, which make up a sizable portion of my stock portfolio and are arguably performing poorly relative to expectations as this war unfolds. I expect gold to continue trading in the $4,500 to $5,500 range until the markets come to realize that central banks, including the Federal Reserve, will not raise rates in response to inflationary pressures. At that point, we can expect the next leg up for gold prices to take shape, with silver likely to tag along to some degree.

Risks to My Thesis

This is an ongoing event; therefore, the outcome may still break in many ways. For instance, I am penciling in a total of 12 weeks of conflict, but the war may end suddenly or last for far longer than most people might expect. There is also a risk that vital oil & gas facilities in the region will be damaged, which means that ramping up production & exports again will probably take far longer than expected. There is also a risk that the markets will react in unexpected ways. I expected the market to be erratic in the first two weeks of this conflict, which is why I decided to largely hunker down and hold my current portfolio position. While I expect more predictable market moves beyond the first few weeks, I could be wrong.

Conclusions

Once in a while, we get events that require a complete overhaul of one's portfolio strategy. Since the start of the second Trump administration, we have had two such events. The tariff tantrum from a year ago, which turned out to be a great buying opportunity, was the first, and now the Iran war. What investors do in response to this event will likely shape their portfolio outcome for the rest of this year & next. The longer it continues, the more profound the effect will be, which is why I am adopting an incremental strategy that ramps up the changes to my portfolio based on how long the conflict lasts. I started with my initial response of doing nothing, largely for the first two weeks. Starting next week, I intend to slowly start taking profits on my ample oil positions and start looking at initial bargains. I intend to increase the magnitude with every week that passes, while continuing to assess the situation as time goes by.
